text: Hello friends, this is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy for large myoma. This patient has a large myoma in the lower posterior right wall of the uterus and it is an intramural myoma.

so now we will start the surgery and here we will do the entry through the palmer's point and according to baseball diamond concept the camera port should be epigastric one lumbar and one contralateral so this is the index finger you will put in the myoma and this is a thumb is epigastric that will be umbilicus one snuff box is one port and another snuff box is another port and the third port is seven point five centimeter lateral
The surgeon creates a small stab wound in the right hypochondriac region using an #11 blade.

The surgeon introduces the Veress needle, angling it towards the stomach. After hearing three clicks indicating entry, a syringe is used to perform an irrigation test, followed by a suction test and a hanging drop test to confirm the needle is inside the abdomen. The abdominal wall is lifted, causing the hanging drop to be sucked down.

The surgeon attaches the tubing to the insufflator, initiating insufflation. The surgical setup follows the baseball diamond concept, with the thumb at the umbilicus and snuff box positions marking port locations.

The insufflator is set to a preset pressure of eighteen, with a flow rate of one liter per minute. The actual pressure is gradually increasing, currently at nineteen, with 1.8 liters of gas already insufflated into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon removes the varus needle and enlarges the incision to 11 mm. An optical trocar is inserted, pointed towards the stomach. The trocar is then removed, and a telescope is introduced into the cannula to begin the initial diagnostic laparoscopy before starting the myomectomy surgery.

The video shows a large intramural myoma located on the posterior wall of the uterus, extending to the anterior wall, measuring 13 centimeters. The fallopian tube and ovary on the right side are visible, along with the intestines.

The surgeon identifies the triangle of doom and triangle of pain, with visible anatomical structures including the right ureter showing peristalsis, the common iliac vessel, external iliac vessel, and sacral promontory. The small bowel is lifted to reveal the aorta, and the infundibulopelvic ligament is observed above the ureter.
The surgeon injects vasopressin diluted in normal saline for hydro dissection. A myoma screw holds the myoma in place as the posterior wall of the uterus is cut longitudinally.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to extend a longitudinal incision approximately six to seven centimeters on the uterine wall. The muscle layers are spread laterally, and a myoma screw is used to secure the fibroid. Suction irrigation is employed to assist in enucleating the fibroid, facilitating blunt dissection of the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon uses a 10 mm stone holding forceps to slowly separate the myoma from surrounding tissues, revealing a clear dissection plane. The myoma is gradually being extracted, reaching up to the falciform ligament on the right side. Muscle fibers obstructing the myoma's removal are divided using a harmonic scalpel to facilitate complete extraction.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to prevent damage to the ascending uterine artery while lifting the myoma with a stone holding forceps. The myoma is carefully maneuvered to avoid damaging the triangle of doom or cecum, and is successfully separated and removed.

The surgeon begins suturing the posterior wall of the uterus, applying mixed mistra knots to the internal muscle layer to approximate the tissue and provide tension. Intracorporeal suturing technique is used to secure the muscle layers.

The surgeon applies a baseball suture using an extracorporeal knot technique to approximate the posterior layer of the uterus. The needle is taken out, and a Mishra's knot is tied with one hitch, followed by a wind and lock sequence to secure the suture.

The surgeon uses a Vandarkar knot pusher to tightly approximate the sutures, applying tension to the muscle layer. The assistant holds the large tail of the suture to facilitate lifting, while additional tension sutures are placed in the deep muscle layer.
The surgeon ties Misra's knot using a Vandarkar knot pusher to secure the tension suture, approximating the muscle layer and including the serous layer for optimal tension over the uterus.

The surgeon applies a third Misra's extracorporeal knot to approximate the tissue, followed by intracorporeal suturing. Visible old blood is collected in the surgical field, but suctioning is deferred until after suturing is complete.

The surgeon begins with a Dhandi jamming knot as a starter suture, followed by continuous suturing along the longitudinal incision on the posterior wall of the uterus. Occasionally, interlocking stitches are applied. The assistant holds the tail of the number one Vicryl suture to facilitate the process, ensuring secure multi-layer closure.

The surgeon terminates the intracorporeal suturing using number one Vicryl after reaching the limit of 20 to 30 centimeters. The tail of the extracorporeal knot is visible, and the intracorporeal continuous suture is tightened. The surgeon prepares to start further suturing with a new suture.

The surgeon applies multiple layers of sutures to the uterine wall, using a combination of extracorporeal and intracorporeal techniques to ensure secure closure of the myoma site. The sutures are carefully placed to prevent potential uterine rupture in future pregnancies.

The surgeon performs deeper suturing on the upper layer of the uterine incision using a continuous suturing technique with a Dandy jamming knot. A pre-tied loop is visible, and the needle is passed through the loop and pulled to lock the suture automatically.

The surgeon completes the final layer of suturing on the uterus, focusing on the serous layer with continuous suturing technique. The ovary and fallopian tube are visible and remain protected throughout the procedure. The surgical field shows minimal blood loss, estimated at 200 to 300 ml, as the myomectomy concludes.

The surgeon uses the tail of the extracorporeal Mishra's knot to terminate the suture, while the assistant holds the ovarian ligament to maintain tension and ensure proper layer approximation. The fallopian tube is carefully avoided during this process.
The surgeon terminates the suture using the Aberdeen termination or Sergius knot, utilizing the long tail left from the previous knot. The suture tail is visibly kept long for this purpose.

The surgeon completes the final C-shaped suture, carefully tightening it to avoid damaging nearby structures. The needle is held due to the short suture length, with caution taken to prevent overshooting and potential injury to the iliac vessel.

After that you can cut it and you can remove it. So suturing is time taking. You need a patience. But it works finally. And nowadays in laparoscopic suturing is equally good as the open surgery. It is nowhere less than that.
The surgeon ties the final knot and cuts the suture, removing the needle using a 5mm port. The procedure transitions to morselation of a 13-centimeter myoma, which is a time-consuming process.

The surgeon uses an electrical morcellator to fragment the large myoma, directing the morcellator towards the anterior abdominal wall for safety. The process is performed carefully to ensure safe removal, particularly in a young patient with no malignancy risk.

The surgeon uses a tenaculum to securely hold the myoma while performing morselation. The morselator is carefully applied to the tissue, ensuring it is brought close to the device for effective morselation. The surgeon proceeds slowly to avoid accidental damage to nearby structures such as the iliac vessel or small bowel.

During the laparoscopic myomectomy, the surgeon carefully performs morselation of the large myoma while monitoring the proximity of the small intestine to ensure safe tissue removal. The procedure requires efficient suturing and management of blood loss, with vasopressin used to control bleeding within its effective timeframe.

so modulation is going on so I should now stop showing you the modulation because it's a very boring process and time taking process and it will continue at least it will take further half an hour to complete the entire morselation.

So as we know by the time we are modulating it just talk about little bit the type of myoma and what are the as you know that size of the myoma is not important. The location is more important as we know that there are three type of myoma that is sub-serious, intramural and sub-mucus. Sub-serious myoma is easiest myoma to perform. and followed by intramural and submucous. So submucous of course you will perform by the hystereoscopy and intramural you have to perform by the laparoscopy with the good suturing skill.
The surgeon carefully extracts the morcellated tissue, ensuring that surrounding bowel structures are not accidentally morcellated. The remaining tissue is prepared for further morcellation.

So sub-serious and pedunculated myoma is easiest myoma to perform because in this you don't have to repair the wall of the uterus because sub-serious one is not making any muscle layer weak but intramural multiple layer of suturing is required that is more challenging. So size may be suppose you have a eighteen centimeter size of myoma but it is a completely sub-serious and pedunculated It will not create any problem for you. You can easily remove it.

But even if you have a three, four centimeter myoma and it is posterior cervical or anterior cervical sitting in between the bladder and the cervix, then you will cry like anything and it will be more difficult. So location is more important. Another important is vascularity.

Vascularity also it is always better to have the complete vascular mapping with the MRI or ultrasound because some of the myoma is so highly vascular that it creates problem. Another question which very commonly asked is should we give the GNRH before doing myomectomy? Generally we don't do so because giving GNRH can of course shrink the myoma but it will make the myoma very soft.

after that it will be very difficult to apply the myoma screw if you will myoma screw if you will put it it will keep on coming out and it will behave like a boiled potato as you can if you put a screw in the boiled potato it will never hold it will come out so exactly same way if a lot of gnrh is given myoma shrinks in the size it may be useful for open surgery because in open surgery this is your beautiful finger Which is acting as for the enucleation.

During the laparoscopic myomectomy, the surgeon uses a tenaculum or myoma screw to hold the myoma, which is challenging due to its softness. The video shows the ongoing morselation process, with the surgeon working on the last part of the myoma, ensuring efficient removal.
The surgeon uses stone holding forceps to remove fragmented tissue from the surgical field following morselation. Final irrigation and suction are performed to clear the area.

The surgeon uses suction to remove collected blood from the surgical field and places a drain into the posterior cul-de-sac. The patient's head is elevated, causing blood from the upper abdomen to move towards the lower abdomen.

The surgeon performs final suction near Morrison's pouch and other areas, followed by the closure of the 15mm port used for the morcellator to prevent hernia formation. The surgery concludes with the port closure.
So thank you very much for watching this video. Have a nice day.
